name: London System
description: The d4–Bf4 system: solid structure, easy plans, hard to break.
source: curated repertoire (scripts/repertoires/london.pgn) + lichess explorer, ratings 1400/1600/1800, speeds blitz/rapid

children.comment: The Stonewall grip; Qf3-h3 and Rf3 lifts are coming.
The London knight: supported by d4 and the g3 bishop, it anchors every attacking plan.
Again: never take on d6 for Black, make Black take on g3 and give you the half-open h-file.
Offer the trade: without queens Black's only active idea is gone.
The pyramid: d4 is overprotected before any action starts.
Keep the bishop: trading on g3 opens the h-file for the rook later.
Back in the main system: the early ...c5 changed nothing.
The knight lands on b5 anyway: Nc7+ ideas force Black to spend moves on defence.
b2 is poisoned: 4...Qxb2?? 5.Nb5! traps the queen against Nc7+ and Rb1.
Also fine; e3 first keeps the move order flexible.
A retreat square: ...Nh5 must never win the bishop pair for free.
Solid: let Black spend time proving ...cxd4 helps anyone.

variations.name: Main system vs ...d5
variations.intro: The London setup against the classical ...d5/...e6/...c5 structure: Bf4 outside the pawn chain, the e3/c3/Nbd2 pyramid, then Ne5 and a kingside plan. Same pieces, same squares, almost every game.

variations.name: vs King's Indian setup
variations.intro: Against ...g6 the London turns positional: keep the pyramid, trade dark squares carefully, expand on the queenside.

variations.name: vs early ...c5
variations.intro: 2...c5 hits d4 before the pyramid exists. Stay calm: e3 and c3 hold the centre, and the London pieces go to their usual squares.
